title: Range Sum Query (Static)
source_statement: Given a static array A of n ≤ 2000 integers, answer Q ≤ 2000 queries. Each query (l, r) asks for the sum of A[l..r].
source_input: First line: n Q. Second line: n integers. Next Q lines: l r.
source_output: Q lines, each the sum A[l..r].
shifted_statement: Given an array A of n ≤ 2×10^5 integers and Q ≤ 2×10^5 operations. Operation type 1: add x to all elements in A[l..r]. Operation type 2: report the maximum element in A[l..r].
shifted_input: First line: n Q. Second line: n integers. Next Q lines: type l r [x].
shifted_output: For each type-2 query, print the maximum.
old_solution_failure: TLE (prefix sum cannot handle range-add updates in O(1); rebuilding is O(n) per update)

title: Counting Pairs with Sum (Quadratic)
source_statement: Given an array A of n ≤ 3000 integers and a target T, count pairs (i,j) with i < j such that A[i] + A[j] = T.
source_input: Line 1: n T. Line 2: n integers.
shifted_statement: Given an array A of n ≤ 10^6 integers and a target T, count pairs (i,j) with i < j such that A[i] + A[j] = T.
shifted_input: Line 1: n T. Line 2: n integers.
old_solution_failure: TLE (O(n²) with n=10^6 gives ~10^12 ops)

title: Shortest Path in Small Graph
source_statement: Given a weighted directed graph with n ≤ 500 nodes and m ≤ 5000 edges, find the shortest path from node 1 to node n.
source_input: Line 1: n m. Next m lines: u v w.
shifted_statement: Given a weighted directed graph with n ≤ 2×10^5 nodes and m ≤ 5×10^5 edges, find the shortest path from node 1 to node n.
shifted_input: Line 1: n m. Next m lines: u v w.
old_solution_failure: TLE (Bellman-Ford O(nm) ≈ 10^10 ops; Floyd O(n³) ≈ 8×10^15 ops)

title: Static Connectivity
source_statement: Given n ≤ 10^5 nodes and m ≤ 2×10^5 edges (added all at once), answer Q ≤ 10^5 queries: are nodes u and v connected?
source_input: Line 1: n m. Next m lines: u v. Line: Q. Next Q lines: u v.
source_output: YES or NO for each query.
shifted_statement: Given n ≤ 2×10^5 nodes and Q ≤ 2×10^5 operations: type 1: add edge (u,v); type 2: delete edge (u,v) (guaranteed to exist); type 3: query whether u and v are connected.
shifted_constraints: 1 ≤ n ≤ 2×10^5; 1 ≤ Q ≤ 2×10^5; all deletions refer to existing edges
shifted_input: Line 1: n Q. Next Q lines: op u v.
shifted_output: YES or NO for each type-3 query.
old_solution_failure: WA (DSU cannot handle deletions; no rollback mechanism)

title: Interval Sum (Static → Point Update)
source_statement: Given array A of n ≤ 10^5 integers, answer Q ≤ 10^5 range-sum queries (l, r).
source_input: Line 1: n Q. Line 2: n integers. Next Q lines: l r.
shifted_statement: Given array A of n ≤ 5×10^5 integers and Q ≤ 5×10^5 operations: type 1: update A[i] += x; type 2: query the sum A[l..r].
shifted_input: Line 1: n Q. Line 2: n integers. Next Q lines: 1 i x OR 2 l r.
shifted_output: For each type-2 query, print the sum.
old_solution_failure: TLE+WA (prefix sum cannot handle point updates without O(n) rebuild)

title: MST → Minimax Path
source_statement: Given a weighted undirected graph with n ≤ 2×10^5 nodes and m ≤ 5×10^5 edges, find the minimum spanning tree weight.
source_input: Line 1: n m. Next m lines: u v w.
shifted_statement: Given a weighted undirected graph with n ≤ 2×10^5 nodes and m ≤ 5×10^5 edges, and Q ≤ 2×10^5 queries. Each query (u, v) asks: what is the minimum possible maximum edge weight on any path from u to v?
shifted_input: Line 1: n m Q. Next m lines: u v w. Next Q lines: u v.
shifted_output: Q lines, each the minimax path value.
old_solution_failure: WA (MST weight ≠ sum of minimax path queries; completely different problem)

title: Max Subarray → Count Positive Subarrays
source_statement: Given array A of n ≤ 10^5 integers, find the maximum subarray sum.
shifted_statement: Given array A of n ≤ 2×10^5 integers, count the number of contiguous subarrays with positive sum (sum > 0).
old_solution_failure: WA (Kadane's finds max subarray, not count of positive subarrays)

title: Interval Scheduling with Switch Budget
source_statement: Given n ≤ 10^5 intervals [s_i, e_i], select the maximum number of non-overlapping intervals.
source_input: Line 1: n. Next n lines: s e.
shifted_statement: Given n ≤ 10^5 intervals [s_i, e_i], each with a color c_i ∈ {1..k}. Select the maximum number of non-overlapping intervals such that the number of consecutive pairs with different colors is at most K.
shifted_input: Line 1: n K. Next n lines: s e c.
shifted_output: Maximum intervals selected satisfying the color-switch constraint.

target_complexity.time: O(n² K) or O(n K log n)

old_solution_failure: WA (greedy by earliest finish ignores global switch budget; may pick intervals that consume too many switches early)

title: Job Scheduling with Cooling Constraint
source_statement: You have n ≤ 10^5 jobs with deadlines d_i and profits p_i. Each job takes 1 unit of time. Schedule jobs to maximize total profit, where a job must be done by its deadline.
source_input: Line 1: n. Next n lines: d p.
shifted_statement: You have n ≤ 10^5 jobs with deadlines d_i, profits p_i, and types t_i ∈ {1..m}. Each job takes 1 unit of time. After scheduling a job of type t, you must wait at least C time slots before scheduling another job of the same type. Schedule to maximize total profit.
shifted_input: Line 1: n m C. Next n lines: d p t.

target_complexity.time: O(n² m) or O(n m log n)

old_solution_failure: WA (greedy ignores cooldown; picks same-type jobs in consecutive slots)

title: Fractional Knapsack → 0/1 Knapsack
source_statement: You have n ≤ 10^4 items with weight w_i and value v_i, and a knapsack of capacity W ≤ 10^6. You can take fractional amounts. Maximize total value.
source_input: Line 1: n W. Next n lines: w v.
shifted_statement: You have n ≤ 500 items with weight w_i and value v_i, and a knapsack of capacity W ≤ 10^4. Each item must be taken whole or not at all. Maximize total value.
shifted_input: Line 1: n W. Next n lines: w v.
old_solution_failure: WA (greedy by v/w is optimal for fractional but not 0/1; e.g., items 1+2 give 160, but 2+3 give 220)

title: Matrix Exponentiation for Fibonacci
source_statement: Compute the n-th Fibonacci number modulo 10^9+7, for n ≤ 10^6.
shifted_statement: Compute the n-th Fibonacci number modulo 10^9+7, for n ≤ 10^18.
old_solution_failure: TLE (O(n) loop with n=10^18 would take ~31 years)

title: Longest Common Subsequence (Small)
source_statement: Given two strings S and T with |S|, |T| ≤ 3000, find their LCS length.
shifted_statement: Given two integer sequences S and T with |S| = |T| = n ≤ 2×10^5, where both sequences are permutations of 1..n, find their LCS length.
shifted_constraints: 1 ≤ n ≤ 2×10^5; S and T are permutations of 1..n
shifted_input: Line 1: n. Line 2: S. Line 3: T.
old_solution_failure: TLE (O(n²) DP with n=2×10^5 gives 4×10^10 ops; special case: permutations allow reduction to LIS)

title: BFS Reachability → Online Edge Insertion Query
source_statement: Given an undirected graph with n ≤ 10^5 nodes and m ≤ 2×10^5 edges (given upfront), answer Q ≤ 10^5 queries: how many nodes are reachable from node u within K hops?
source_input: Line 1: n m. Next m lines: u v. Line: Q. Next Q lines: u K.
shifted_statement: Start with an empty graph of n ≤ 10^5 nodes. Process Q ≤ 2×10^5 operations: type 1: add edge (u,v); type 2: given node u, query the size of its connected component.
shifted_input: Line 1: n Q. Next Q lines: 1 u v OR 2 u.
shifted_output: For each type-2 query, component size.
old_solution_failure: TLE (BFS per query O(Q(n+m)) is too slow for online edge insertions)

title: Two Sum (Large N)
source_statement: Given an array of 1000 integers and a target T, find any two distinct indices i<j such that A[i]+A[j]=T. Output the indices (1-indexed).
source_input:
First line: n T
Second line: n integers
shifted_statement: Given an array of up to 50000 integers and a target T, find any two distinct indices i<j such that A[i]+A[j]=T. Output the indices (1-indexed), or -1 if none.
shifted_input:
First line: n T
Second line: n integers
old_solution_failure: TLE: O(n^2) times out for n=50000 in Python within 5s

title: Pattern Matching (Large Text)
source_statement: Given a text of length n ≤ 1000 and pattern p of length m ≤ 50, find all starting positions (1-indexed) of p in text.
shifted_statement: Given a text of length n ≤ 200000 and pattern p of length m ≤ 1000, find all starting positions (1-indexed) of p in text.
old_solution_failure: TLE: O(nm) times out for n=200000, m=1000

title: Range Update Range Sum Query
source_statement: Given a static array of n ≤ 1000 integers, answer q ≤ 1000 range-sum queries [l,r].
source_input:
Line 1: n q
Line 2: n integers
Next q lines: l r
shifted_statement: Given an array of n ≤ 300000 integers, process q ≤ 300000 operations: either ADD l r v (add v to all A[l..r]) or QUERY l r (output sum of A[l..r]).
shifted_input:
Line 1: n q
Line 2: n integers
Next q lines: ADD l r v  or  QUERY l r
old_solution_failure: TLE: naive O(nq) times out for n=q=300000

title: Shortest Path (Negative Weights)
source_statement: Given a directed graph with n nodes, m edges (all non-negative weights), find shortest path from node 1 to node n.
source_input:
Line 1: n m
Next m lines: u v w
shifted_statement: Given a directed graph with n nodes, m edges (weights may be negative, no negative cycles), find shortest path from node 1 to node n.
shifted_constraints: 1 ≤ n ≤ 1000, 1 ≤ m ≤ 5000, -10^6 ≤ w ≤ 10^6, no negative cycles
shifted_input:
Line 1: n m
Next m lines: u v w
old_solution_failure: WA: Dijkstra gives wrong answer when negative edges exist

title: Weighted Job Scheduling
source_statement: Given n jobs each with start time, end time, select maximum number of non-overlapping jobs. Output the count.
source_input:
Line 1: n
Next n lines: start end
shifted_statement: Given n jobs each with start time, end time, and weight, find the maximum total weight of non-overlapping jobs. Output the maximum weight.
shifted_input:
Line 1: n
Next n lines: start end weight
old_solution_failure: WA: greedy by earliest end time ignores weights

title: Coin Change (Non-Canonical Denominations)
source_statement: Given coin denominations [1,5,10,25] (canonical), make exact change for amount S using minimum coins.
shifted_statement: Given arbitrary coin denominations (not necessarily canonical) and amount S, make exact change using minimum coins, or -1 if impossible.
shifted_input:
Line 1: k
Line 2: k denominations
Line 3: S
old_solution_failure: WA: greedy largest-first fails for non-canonical denominations

title: Range Minimum Query (Sparse Table)
source_statement: Given an array arr and a list of queries, each query (l,r) asks for the minimum of arr[l..r]. Return a list of answers.
shifted_statement: Given an array arr of n integers and a list of q queries, each query (l,r) asks for the minimum of arr[l..r]. n and q may be up to 100000; O(n·q) is too slow. Use a sparse table for O(n log n) build + O(1) per query. Return a list of answers.
shifted_output: list of minimums, same length as queries
old_solution_failure: TLE: O(n·q) = 10^10 when n=q=10^5

title: 0/1 Knapsack (Greedy Trap)
source_statement: Given n items (weights w, values v) and knapsack capacity W, maximise total value. Use greedy: sort by value/weight ratio.
source_input: solve(W, items) → int  # items = list of [weight, value]
shifted_statement: Given n items each with weight and value, and knapsack capacity W. Items are indivisible (0/1 knapsack). The greedy ratio approach gives the wrong answer. Use dynamic programming.
shifted_input: solve(W, items) → int  # items = list of [weight, value]
old_solution_failure: WA: greedy picks w=1,v=1 (ratio 1.0) over w=10,v=9 (ratio 0.9), missing optimal answer 9

title: Edit Distance (Levenshtein)
source_statement: Compute the minimum edit distance between two short strings s and t (insertions, deletions, substitutions).
shifted_statement: Compute the minimum edit distance between two strings s and t (up to length 1000). The naive recursive solution is too slow. Use the Wagner-Fischer DP algorithm.
old_solution_failure: TLE: recursive O(3^n) times out for strings of length 20+

title: Fibonacci (Exponential→DP)
source_statement: Return the n-th Fibonacci number using naive recursion.
shifted_statement: Return the n-th Fibonacci number. n can be up to 10^6; naive recursion is far too slow. Use iterative DP.
old_solution_failure: TLE: recursive Fib(40) takes ~2s; Fib(10^6) impossible

title: Fast Exponentiation
source_statement: Compute base^exp mod m using a loop that multiplies base exp times.
shifted_statement: Compute base^exp mod m. exp can be up to 10^18; a linear loop is too slow. Use fast (binary) exponentiation.
old_solution_failure: TLE: loop runs 10^18 times

title: Count Inversions (Merge Sort)
source_statement: Count the number of inversions in an array: pairs (i,j) where i<j but arr[i]>arr[j]. Use nested loops.
shifted_statement: Count inversions in an array of up to 10^5 elements. O(n^2) is too slow. Use merge sort to count during merging.

title: Binary Search (Linear→Log)
source_statement: Given a sorted array and a target, find the index of target using linear scan. Return -1 if not found.
shifted_statement: Given a sorted array of up to 10^8 elements and a target, find its index. Linear scan is too slow. Use binary search.
shifted_constraints: 1 ≤ n ≤ 10^8 (given as sorted list in tests), array is strictly sorted
old_solution_failure: TLE: linear scan on 10^8 elements

title: GCD (Subtraction→Euclidean)
source_statement: Compute GCD of two numbers using repeated subtraction.
shifted_statement: Compute GCD of two numbers, where one may be up to 10^18. Repeated subtraction is far too slow. Use the Euclidean algorithm.
old_solution_failure: TLE: subtraction needs up to 10^18 steps

title: Anagram Check (Sort→Hash)
source_statement: Check if two strings are anagrams by sorting both and comparing.
shifted_statement: Check if two strings are anagrams. n up to 10^6; O(n log n) sort is acceptable but a hash-based O(n) counter is required for very tight time limits.
old_solution_failure: TLE on very large strings with tight time limit requiring O(n)

title: String Pattern Search (Naive→KMP)
source_statement: Find the first occurrence index of pattern p in text s using naive character comparison.
source_output: start index of first occurrence or -1
shifted_statement: Find the first occurrence of pattern p in text s. Both can be up to 10^6 chars; naive O(nm) is too slow. Use KMP or Z-algorithm for O(n+m).
old_solution_failure: TLE: O(nm) = 10^12 for n=m=10^6

title: Sliding Window Maximum
source_statement: Given an array and window size k, find the maximum in each window using a simple loop.
shifted_statement: Find the maximum in each sliding window of size k. n up to 10^5 and k up to n; O(nk) is too slow. Use a monotonic deque for O(n).

title: Longest Substring Without Repeating
source_statement: Find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters using brute force.
shifted_statement: Find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters. n up to 10^5; O(n^2) too slow. Use a sliding window with a hash set for O(n).
old_solution_failure: TLE: O(n^2) for n=10^5

title: Trapping Rain Water
source_statement: Compute trapped rain water between bars. For each position, find left/right max with nested loops.
shifted_statement: Compute trapped rain water for up to 10^5 bars. O(n^2) too slow. Use two-pointer technique for O(n).
old_solution_failure: TLE: O(n^2) for n=10^5

title: Maximum Subarray (Kadane)
source_statement: Find the maximum sum subarray using a nested loop over all pairs.
shifted_statement: Find the maximum sum contiguous subarray. n up to 10^6; O(n^2) too slow. Use Kadane's algorithm O(n).
old_solution_failure: TLE: O(n^2) for n=10^6

title: Two Sum (O(n²)→Hash)
source_statement: Find two indices in arr that sum to target using nested loops.
shifted_statement: Find two indices that sum to target. n up to 10^6; nested loops too slow. Use a hash map for O(n).
old_solution_failure: TLE: O(n^2) for n=10^6

title: LRU Cache (Array→OrderedDict)
source_statement: Implement get/put for an LRU cache using a list (linear eviction scan).
source_input: solve(capacity, ops) → list  # ops: list of ('get',k) or ('put',k,v); return list of get results (-1 if miss)
shifted_statement: Implement LRU cache with O(1) get and put. Capacity up to 10^4, up to 10^6 operations. Use OrderedDict or doubly-linked-list + hash map.
old_solution_failure: TLE: O(n) per operation × 10^6 ops = 10^10

title: Coin Change (Min Coins, Greedy→DP)
source_statement: Find minimum coins to make amount using greedy (always pick largest coin ≤ remaining).
shifted_statement: Find minimum coins to make amount with arbitrary coin denominations. Greedy fails for non-canonical coins. Use DP.
shifted_constraints: 1 ≤ len(coins) ≤ 12, 1 ≤ amount ≤ 10000, coins may be non-canonical
old_solution_failure: WA: greedy with coins=[1,3,4] and amount=6 gives 1+1+4=3 coins, optimal is 3+3=2 coins

title: Longest Increasing Subsequence (O(n²)→O(n log n))
source_statement: Find the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence using O(n^2) DP.
shifted_statement: Find LIS length. n up to 10^5; O(n^2) DP too slow. Use patience sorting with binary search for O(n log n).
old_solution_failure: TLE: O(n^2) for n=10^5

title: Word Break (Exponential→DP)
source_statement: Determine if string s can be segmented into dictionary words using naive recursion.
source_output: True if s can be segmented
shifted_statement: Determine if s (up to 300 chars) can be segmented into dict words. Naive recursion TLEs. Use DP with a boolean array.

source_statement: Determine if you can reach the last index of the array from index 0. Each element is the max jump length. Use brute force DFS.

title: House Robber (Greedy→DP)
source_statement: Rob houses for maximum money; no two adjacent. Greedy: pick every other house greedily.
shifted_statement: Rob houses for maximum money; no two adjacent. Greedy fails on some distributions. Use DP.
old_solution_failure: WA: greedy with [2,7,9,3,1] picks 2,9,1=12 but optimal is 2+9+1=12 (happens to match), harder: [2,1,1,2] greedy=4, but optimal=4. Real trap: [1,2,9,4,5,0,4,11,6] greedy alt misses optimal

title: Number of Islands (BFS/DFS)

shifted_statement: Count islands in a large grid. Rows,cols up to 300. Use BFS/DFS for O(rows*cols).
old_solution_failure: TLE: naive re-scan for each visited cell

title: BFS Shortest Path (Unweighted)

source_input: solve(n, edges, src, dst) → int  # edges: list of [u,v]; return -1 if no path
shifted_statement: Find shortest path in unweighted undirected graph. n,m up to 10^5. DFS finds A path but not the shortest. Use BFS.

title: Detect Cycle (Directed Graph)

title: Topological Sort

shifted_output: valid topological order (any valid answer accepted)
